Buy-In Business Portfolio · Case Files

Franchise Case Files

All 656 buy-in businesses, each as its own deep-dive file. Tap any to open the full breakdown - fee, total cost, royalty, track record, owner profit, staffing, customer acquisition, and territory - with your gate, survival, model-type, and hands-off tags up top. Figures are screening estimates; the definitive numbers live in each brand's FDD.

656 files 219 clear both gates 4 volumes Vol I-IV

Viewing your Removed bin. These are hidden from the main list. Tap ↶ Restore on any card to bring it back. Tap Removed again to return.
CASE FILE 001▶

Amazon DSP

Routes & distributorships · Vol I TOP FIT
Buy-in fee
no franchise fee
All-in cost
~$10K start
Royalty+ad
n/a - contract-based
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
$30K
CONTRACT GM-ABLE SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: liquid $30K
01WHAT IT IS
Amazon last-mile delivery contractor program (not a franchise); operators run branded van fleets delivering packages, with 3,000+ DSPs nationwide.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: no franchise fee. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around ~$10K start. The buy-in fee alone is no franchise fee, with ongoing royalty/ad running n/a - contract-based. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - contract-based. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

Buy-in fee: $0. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $250K-$1.5M+. The buy-in fee alone is $0, with ongoing royalty/ad running n/a - contract-based. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - contract-based. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.

Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

DISTRIB HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
Known gate figures: liquid 10-30% down
01WHAT IT IS
Independent distributor routes reselling packaged bread (Flowers/Bimbo brands) to stores on a protected territory; margin-based, not a franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: distributorship buy-in. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $55K-$500K. The buy-in fee alone is distributorship buy-in, with ongoing royalty/ad running n/a - product markup. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - product markup. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

02INITIAL FEE

03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE

Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. 04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL

CASE FILE 005▶

Matco Tools (tool truck)

Routes & distributorships · Vol I GOOD FIT
Buy-in fee
$8K
All-in cost
$77K-$314K
Royalty+ad
none
Net worth req
~$35K
Liquid req
$25-40K
FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~90%/yr TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $35-75K; liquid $15-40K
01WHAT IT IS
Mobile automotive tool distributor route (Vontier); you sell tools from a truck to mechanics on a protected call list, with 1,600+ distributors.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $8K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $77K-$314K. The buy-in fee alone is $8K and there is no standard percentage royalty. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
No standard percentage royalty indicated - confirm in FDD Item 6 (some brands use flat monthly or per-unit fees instead).
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $200K-$500K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Vending machine route

Routes & distributorships · Vol I SIDE INCOME
Buy-in fee
route purchase price
All-in cost
$2-10K/machine
Royalty+ad
n/a - markup on product
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
ROUTE ABSENTEE-OK SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
01WHAT IT IS
Buy or build a route of vending machines placed in offices/retail; income from product markup, not a franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: route purchase price. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $2-10K/machine. The buy-in fee alone is route purchase price, with ongoing royalty/ad running n/a - markup on product. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Semi-passive or manager-run from early on. One of the more genuinely absentee-friendly models here - though a good manager is still everything.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - markup on product. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

CASE FILE 010▶

Amazon FBA (self-run)

Productized / logistics · Vol I CAUTION
Buy-in fee
none
All-in cost
$500-$10K
Royalty+ad
n/a - self-built
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
SELF-START HANDS-ON SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NONE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
01WHAT IT IS
Sell private-label or resale products through Amazon's Fulfillment-by-Amazon program; a self-run e-commerce business, not a franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: none. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $500-$10K. The buy-in fee alone is none, with ongoing royalty/ad running n/a - self-built. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - self-built. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

Airbnb arbitrage programs

Productized / logistics · Vol I AVOID
Buy-in fee
package price (varies)
All-in cost
Varies + deposits
Royalty+ad
none / not a franchise
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
BIZ-OPP HANDS-ON SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NONE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID n/a
Known gate figures: liquid Cash + leases
01WHAT IT IS
Lease properties and re-list them as short-term rentals for the spread; a self-run model, not a franchise, with real legal/lease risk.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: package price (varies). This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around Varies + deposits. The buy-in fee alone is package price (varies), with ongoing royalty/ad running none / not a franchise. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: none / not a franchise. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.
CASE FILE 013▶

Christmas Decor

Home-services bolt-ons & franchises · Vol I TOP FIT
Buy-in fee
$9,900
All-in cost
$19.5K-$62K
Royalty+ad
~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $100K; liquid $50K
01WHAT IT IS
Holiday lighting. Counter-seasonal, reuses crews/trucks. Fee $9,900; ~$226K avg gross, ~15% margin, ~2.5-yr payback.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $9,900. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $19.5K-$62K. The buy-in fee alone is $9,900, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Landscaping and lawn-maintenance franchise (Neighborly).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $75K-$253K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Neighborly/portfolio-style manager-run model; owning other non-competing brands is common. In-term non-compete covers competing services only.

Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.

Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.

08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Manager-run cleaning; competing-cleaning non-compete only.
CASE FILE 034▶

The Cleaning Authority

Cleaning (specialty / residential / commercial) · Vol I GOOD FIT
Buy-in fee
$40K
All-in cost
$102K-$169K
Royalty+ad
6%
Net worth req
$250K
Liquid req
$100K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH n/a LIQUID ~
Known gate figures: liquid ~$50K
01WHAT IT IS
Residential cleaning franchise with a detailed rotation system.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $102K-$169K. The buy-in fee alone is $40K, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Manager-run cleaning; competing-cleaning non-compete only.

05OWNER PROFIT

06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

CASE FILE 047▶

H&R Block

Tax prep (counter-seasonal) · Vol I GOOD FIT
Buy-in fee
est. $25-50K
All-in cost
$32K-$158K
Royalty+ad
~5-6% + 1-2% (est.)
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL HIGH ~92% TERR: RADIUS OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $100K; liquid $50K
01WHAT IT IS
Counter-seasonal. Tiny fee (~$2,500), high royalty, ~8% failure. Mostly buy an existing office.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $32K-$158K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $1.47M-$2.73M. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Demanding owner-operator model (no passive investors), but nothing bars you from owning other unrelated franchises.

03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $427K-$2.3M. The buy-in fee alone is $10K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
The one brand that bars a portfolio: requires a full-time, hands-on operator with no other active business ventures.

CASE FILE 062▶

MLMs (Mary Kay, Avon, Herbalife, Amway...)

MLM / direct sales · Vol I AVOID
Buy-in fee
starter kit
All-in cost
$50-$500 kit
Royalty+ad
n/a - commission
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
MLM HANDS-ON SURVIVAL VERY LOW TERR: NONE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Cheapest entry, worst odds. FTC: most participants make under $1,000/yr.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: starter kit. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50-$500 kit. The buy-in fee alone is starter kit, with ongoing royalty/ad running n/a - commission. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival signal is POOR here (low / shrinking / defunct) - treat as high-risk and dig hard before committing. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: n/a - commission. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Confirm what marketing the company actually funds versus what falls on you.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $150-300K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running 4%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 4%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Known gate figures: liquid $75K
01WHAT IT IS
HVAC repair and installation franchise (Authority Brands) known for on-time guarantees.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $85-224K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Semi-absentee/manager-run home-service brand; own other non-competing businesses freely.

Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 2.5%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

CASE FILE 077▶

Fence Dynamics

Garage / Gutter / Fence / Floors · Vol II
Buy-in fee
$40K
All-in cost
$110-154K
Royalty+ad
6%
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH n/a LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: liquid $50K
01WHAT IT IS
Fence install; no industry experience required.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $110-154K. The buy-in fee alone is $40K, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH n/a LIQUID n/a
01WHAT IT IS
Water, fire and mold restoration franchise (Neighborly).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $59K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $67-226K. The buy-in fee alone is $59K, with ongoing royalty/ad running 7%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 7%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
Known gate figures: NW req $75K; liquid $30K
01WHAT IT IS
Handyman express model. Clears both gates.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $45K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $90-116K. The buy-in fee alone is $45K, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS

02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $81-195K. The buy-in fee alone is $40K, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Express tunnel car-wash franchise with a high-throughput automated model.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $3.5-6M. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Semi-passive or manager-run from early on. One of the more genuinely absentee-friendly models here - though a good manager is still everything.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Semi-absentee / manager-run, and the non-compete covers competing businesses only - it slots cleanly into a multi-brand portfolio.

01WHAT IT IS
Fast-casual cafe serving smoothies and food; ~1,600 US cafes (acquired by Blackstone in 2024).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $300-815K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-involved (a 2-unit semi-absentee path is marketed); non-compete blocks competing concepts only.

Buy-in fee: $40K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $692K-1.52M. The buy-in fee alone is $40K, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Hawaiian coffee/smoothies; cart-truck-cafe, no royalty/marketing fees.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around flexible/mobile. The buy-in fee alone is $25K and there is no standard percentage royalty. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
No standard percentage royalty indicated - confirm in FDD Item 6 (some brands use flat monthly or per-unit fees instead).
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Buy-in fee: $35K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $484-700K. The buy-in fee alone is $35K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-involved bakery, multi-unit friendly; competing-bakery non-compete only.

01WHAT IT IS
Mobile pet-grooming franchise operating from custom vans.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $100-180K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-operated mobile grooming; own other non-competing brands freely.

In-home senior care; low barriers.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $77-166K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 8%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Known gate figures: NW req $60-150K; liquid $100K
01WHAT IT IS
Business brokerage and franchise-consulting franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $97-131K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Broker model, owner-led; competing-brokerage non-compete only.

Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req

BIZ-OPP HANDS-ON SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NONE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH n/a LIQUID n/a
01WHAT IT IS
Medical-billing business; home-based, training-led.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: package price (varies). This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around low. The buy-in fee alone is package price (varies), with ongoing royalty/ad running none / not a franchise. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: none / not a franchise. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

01WHAT IT IS
Residential property-management franchise (Neighborly).
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $92-234K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Property-management model, commonly absentee-friendly; competing-PM non-compete only.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
Known gate figures: NW req $300K; liquid $150K
01WHAT IT IS
Group fitness bootcamp franchise focused on women.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: est. $25-50K. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $150-407K. The buy-in fee alone is est. $25-50K, with ongoing royalty/ad running ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: ~5-6% + 1-2% (est.). This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.
