Royalty+ad
none
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
BIZ-OPP GM-ABLE SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NONE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
CAUTION: Continuous concrete curbing; non-FDD business-opportunity seller - verify hard.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $25K-$60K. The buy-in fee alone is varies and there is no standard percentage royalty. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
No standard percentage royalty indicated - confirm in FDD Item 6 (some brands use flat monthly or per-unit fees instead).
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
NO territory protection. Others can operate right beside you.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.
CASE FILE 452▶

The Gutter Boys

Lawn / Deck / Fence / Concrete (new) · Vol IV CLEARS
Buy-in fee
$25,000
All-in cost
$50K-$120K
Royalty+ad
6%
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL HIGH ~85%+ TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Gutter install/clean/guards; low entry, recurring cleaning.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50K-$120K. The buy-in fee alone is $25,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Grout/tile/caulk restoration; owner-operator, very low overhead.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $30K-$70K. The buy-in fee alone is $25,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 5%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 5%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6% + 2%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Carpet dyeing/color restoration; niche, low overhead.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $40K-$90K. The buy-in fee alone is $25,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

dealer/varies
Royalty+ad
varies
Net worth req
$300K
Liquid req
$100K
DEALER GM-ABLE SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NEGOTIATED MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
Foundation repair and stabilization franchise/dealer network.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around dealer/varies. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is NEGOTIATED as a dealer - there may be no formal protection at all. Insist on written terms.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

Power-washing franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $50,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $200K-$450K. The buy-in fee alone is $50,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6% + 2%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6% + 2%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Mobile grill/BBQ cleaning; ultra-low overhead, niche recurring.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $10,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $15K-$40K. The buy-in fee alone is $10,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Buy-in fee
varies
All-in cost
$50K-$700K
Royalty+ad
varies
Net worth req
$500K
Liquid req
$200K
DEALER GM-ABLE SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NEGOTIATED OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH ~ LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
Replacement window and exterior-remodeling franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50K-$700K. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is NEGOTIATED as a dealer - there may be no formal protection at all. Insist on written terms.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-led exterior remodeling; competing-business non-compete only.

Security-patrol services; B2B contracts, scalable.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $52K-$220K. The buy-in fee alone is $40,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 7%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 7%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.
CASE FILE 487▶

ADT Authorized Dealer

Locksmith / Security / Safety · Vol IV CLEARS
Buy-in fee
none
All-in cost
low start
Royalty+ad
varies
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
DEALER GM-ABLE SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NEGOTIATED MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Alarm/smart-home dealer; installs/monitoring - dealer program, not FDD.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: none. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around low start. The buy-in fee alone is none, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is NEGOTIATED as a dealer - there may be no formal protection at all. Insist on written terms.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

01WHAT IT IS
Pack, ship, print and mailbox retail franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $30,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $180K-$500K. The buy-in fee alone is $30,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 5% + 2.5%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 5% + 2.5%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Active hands-on ownership with an on-site primary operator; competing-business non-compete only.

Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.
CASE FILE 498▶

TeamLogic IT

Mailbox / Print / IT / Cell Repair · Vol IV
Buy-in fee
$55,000
All-in cost
$105K-$160K
Royalty+ad
4-7%
Net worth req
$250K
Liquid req
$100K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: RADIUS OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH NO LIQUID ~
01WHAT IT IS
Managed IT services franchise serving small and mid-size businesses.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $55,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $105K-$160K. The buy-in fee alone is $55,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 4-7%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 4-7%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Executive-owner IT model; own other non-competing brands freely.

FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Shared direct-mail advertising; home-based, recurring ad clients. SEO/sales fit.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $45,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $58K-$97K. The buy-in fee alone is $45,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Ongoing: 6% + 1%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 5% + 2%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.

Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

01WHAT IT IS
Senior move management; asset-light, referral-driven.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around low. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

01WHAT IT IS
Buy/build an agency; needs ~$100K liquid, no franchise fee.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: none (buy book). This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around buy-in varies. The buy-in fee alone is none (buy book), with ongoing royalty/ad running commission. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: commission. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Carrier brand plus your local grind. Referral partnerships (mortgage, real-estate, community networking) drive the book; the company supplies product access and credibility, not clients.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: flat-fee. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.

Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $150K-$450K. The buy-in fee alone is $35,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 5-8%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 5-8%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

varies

$30K-$150K
Royalty+ad
varies
Net worth req
n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
DEALER GM-ABLE SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NEGOTIATED MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Protective-coating dealer; low entry option, equipment-based.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $30K-$150K. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is NEGOTIATED as a dealer - there may be no formal protection at all. Insist on written terms.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

01WHAT IT IS
Mobile bike sales/service; van-based, niche.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $25,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $80K-$150K. The buy-in fee alone is $25,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 8%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 8%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

$65,000
All-in cost
$65K-$150K
Royalty+ad
% of GP
Net worth req
$150K
Liquid req
$65K
FRANCHISE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: OPEN OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH ~ LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Shipping/logistics franchise; resell discounted carrier shipping to businesses, earning a share of the margin.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $65,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $65K-$150K. The buy-in fee alone is $65,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running % of GP. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: % of GP. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

01WHAT IT IS
Pack, ship, print and business-services retail franchise.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $35,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $180K-$260K. The buy-in fee alone is $35,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 5%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 5%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Generally OPEN / non-exclusive - no territory protection; you compete with other operators of the same brand.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

Dropoff (courier)

n/a - not a franchise
Liquid req
varies
CONTRACT HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: CONTRACT TERR MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Same-day B2B courier; logistics fit for your background.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around varies. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
No standard percentage royalty indicated - confirm in FDD Item 6 (some brands use flat monthly or per-unit fees instead).
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.

02INITIAL FEE

03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50K-$300K. The buy-in fee alone is n/a and there is no standard percentage royalty. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
No standard percentage royalty indicated - confirm in FDD Item 6 (some brands use flat monthly or per-unit fees instead).
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Customer demand is essentially built in - the product brand or the operating contract supplies the volume. That's what you're paying for. Your job is service and retention, not lead generation.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is defined by your route/operating CONTRACT, and may be adjustable by the counterparty. Get the boundaries in writing.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

varies
Net worth req
not published - verify FDD
Liquid req
verify FDD
FRANCHISE ABSENTEE-OK SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Office-coffee service; semi-passive, recurring, B2B placements.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $30,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $50K-$120K. The buy-in fee alone is $30,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Semi-passive or manager-run from early on. One of the more genuinely absentee-friendly models here - though a good manager is still everything.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Semi-absentee / manager-run, and the non-compete covers competing businesses only - it slots cleanly into a multi-brand portfolio.

DEALER HANDS-ON SURVIVAL UNKNOWN - no FDD TERR: NEGOTIATED MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Water delivery/dealer; recurring, route-based.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around low-mid. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Survival is UNKNOWN - no FDD means no Item 20 unit data exists. You can't measure the failure rate from outside. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Territory is NEGOTIATED as a dealer - there may be no formal protection at all. Insist on written terms.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not a traditional franchise - no franchise covenant restricts owning other businesses.

Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.
CASE FILE 558▶

Little Caesars

Food - QSR / Pizza / Sub / Mexican · Vol IV
Buy-in fee
$20,000
All-in cost
$384K-$1.4M
Royalty+ad
6% + ad
Net worth req
$400K
Liquid req
$200K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED ~60-75% TERR: RADIUS MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
01WHAT IT IS
Value pizza QSR with ~4,200 US locations, known for Hot-N-Ready pizza.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $20,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $384K-$1.4M. The buy-in fee alone is $20,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6% + ad. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6% + ad. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Offers a semi-absentee model with an approved on-site operator; competing-pizza non-compete only.

FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL MIXED - saturation TERR: RADIUS OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH NO LIQUID NO
01WHAT IT IS
Gourmet cookie franchise with a rotating weekly menu; ~1,000+ US locations.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $50,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $390K-$690K. The buy-in fee alone is $50,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 8% + 2%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 8% + 2%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Not for absentee owners - you run the first ~60 days - but no bar on owning unrelated franchises.

02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $32,500. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $234K-$477K. The buy-in fee alone is $32,500, with ongoing royalty/ad running 6%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Ongoing: 6%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $45,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $150K-$450K. The buy-in fee alone is $45,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Licensed-clinician-dependent; own other non-competing brands freely.

Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

British Swim School

Kids - Sports / Swim / Music / Martial Arts · Vol IV CLEARS
Buy-in fee
$40,000
All-in cost
$95K-$130K
Royalty+ad
8% + 2%
Net worth req
$100K
Liquid req
$50K
FRANCHISE GM-ABLE SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: EXCLUSIVE MULTI-BRAND OK NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Learn-to-swim franchise operating in host pools with low overhead.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: $40,000. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $95K-$130K. The buy-in fee alone is $40,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running 8% + 2%. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: 8% + 2%. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.

Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.

03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around $250K-$500K. The buy-in fee alone is $40,000, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival is MIXED (~60-75%) - thinner margins and higher churn; validate unit trends carefully. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Local lead generation - your own ad spend, reviews, networking and referrals - is on you. Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Confirm whether there's any real lead-assignment or call-center support (most don't have it) and what the required local marketing minimum is.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Usually only a protected RADIUS around your location - the brand can place other units in the same metro and compete for the same customers.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
Owner-run early then delegable to a manager; the non-compete covers competing businesses only, so you can hold it alongside other unrelated brands.

Owner-run for roughly years 1-3, then realistically delegable to a general manager. This is the fit for a portfolio/hands-off endgame.

Most franchisors give you the tools but you still bring the customers: a branded website, some national/ brand advertising funded by the ad fee, marketing templates, and training. Category survival tends to run HIGH (~85%+) - low fixed overhead means bad years bend rather than break. No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.

LICENSE HANDS-ON SURVIVAL GOOD ~75-85% TERR: EXCLUSIVE OWN OK · HANDS-ON NET WORTH YES LIQUID YES
01WHAT IT IS
Backyard-chicken rental; seasonal niche, very low entry.
02INITIAL FEE
Buy-in fee: varies. This is the franchise/license fee only (FDD Item 5) - not the full cost to open.
03COSTS BEYOND THE FEE
All-in cost lands around low license. The buy-in fee alone is varies, with ongoing royalty/ad running varies. Everything between the fee and the all-in number is what the franchisor does NOT cover - equipment, vehicle, any build-out, signage, tech, licensing, insurance, training travel, and roughly three months of working capital. Pull FDD Item 7 for the exact line-item table.
04TRACK RECORD + SURVIVAL
Category survival tends to run GOOD (~75-85%). No reliable per-brand success rate is published anywhere; the real number is FDD Item 20 - count units opened versus closed/transferred over three years and do that math before signing.
05OWNER PROFIT
Owner take-home depends on scale and how much you run yourself. Ask for FDD Item 19 (the earnings disclosure) and call current and former operators - never trust a headline average.
06STAFFING / HANDS-OFF
Expect to BE the operator. This stays a job you own unless you scale to multiple units/routes and layer in managers. Not a passive play.
07ROYALTY + AD FEES
Ongoing: varies. This comes off the top of gross sales, permanently. Confirm current rates in FDD Item 6.
08CUSTOMER ACQUISITION
Little to no consumer marketing engine comes with this - you generate demand yourself. Treat customer acquisition like a self-start: your website/SEO, local ads, and relationships do the work.
09MULTIPLE OPERATORS IN ONE CITY?
Typically grants an EXCLUSIVE, protected territory - but confirm the size in FDD Item 12. A city can hold several territories, so 'exclusive' is not always one-per-city.
10OWN OTHER FRANCHISES / BUSINESSES?
A franchise's non-compete blocks only competing businesses, so you can own other unrelated brands - but this one is hands-on, so each needs your daily time.
